description: Ranking diagnoses by actual likelihood based on patient demographics, epidemiology, and Bayesian updating as new information arrives — not treating all possibilities equally.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with sudden onset substernal chest pressure.

demographics.text: 22-year-old female, no PMH, regular exercise.
55-year-old male, smoker, HTN, family history of CAD.
80-year-old female, history of diabetes and prior MI.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Over-estimating MI risk in young, healthy patients; under-estimating MI risk in the elderly with non-specific symptoms.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Age and risk factors significantly shift the pre-test probability of MI from near-zero to the primary hypothesis.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with sudden onset right-sided weakness.

demographics.text: 25-year-old female with history of complex migraines.
65-year-old male with atrial fibrillation and HTN.
45-year-old male, diabetic, missed breakfast.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Ignoring hypoglycemia in diabetics; over-diagnosing stroke in young migraineurs.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Atrial fibrillation is a massive multiplier for stroke probability, while diabetes shifts the focus toward metabolic mimics.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with right lower quadrant abdominal pain.

demographics.text: 19-year-old male, previously healthy.
24-year-old female, sexually active, missed period.
75-year-old female with history of diverticulosis.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Missing ectopic pregnancy in females; ignoring diverticulitis in the elderly.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: RLQ pain in a young male is appendicitis until proven otherwise, but in a young female, ectopic pregnancy is equally likely.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with sudden onset shortness of breath.

demographics.text: 30-year-old female on oral contraceptives, recent long flight.
60-year-old male, smoker, history of COPD.
45-year-old male, post-op day 3 from hip replacement.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Under-estimating PE in COPD patients; over-diagnosing panic attacks in young women.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Provocative factors (OCPs, travel, surgery) dramatically increase the probability of PE.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with cough and fever.

demographics.text: 20-year-old college student, living in a dorm.
85-year-old nursing home resident with dementia.
40-year-old male, smoker, history of asthma.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Missing pneumonia in the elderly (who may only show confusion); over-diagnosing pneumonia in young adults with viral URIs.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Institutional living (nursing homes) is a major risk factor that shifts the probability toward pneumonia.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with headache and fever.

demographics.text: 19-year-old college student, not vaccinated for meningococcus.
45-year-old female with history of chronic migraines.
2-year-old child, incomplete vaccinations.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Ignoring meningitis in vaccinated patients (though risk is lower, it's not zero); over-diagnosing meningitis in migraineurs.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Vaccination status and age (extremes) are the primary drivers of meningitis probability.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with unilateral leg swelling.

demographics.text: 35-year-old male, active, no PMH.
60-year-old female, active cancer, recent chemotherapy.
50-year-old male, post-op day 10 from knee surgery.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Missing DVT in cancer patients; over-diagnosing DVT in young athletes with muscle strains.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Malignancy and recent surgery are the strongest predictors of DVT probability.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with hypotension and tachycardia.

demographics.text: 20-year-old male, marathon runner, just finished a race.
80-year-old female with indwelling catheter and fever.
45-year-old male, diabetic, with a large infected foot ulcer.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Attributing hypotension solely to dehydration in an elderly patient with a clear infection source.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: The presence of an infection source (catheter/ulcer) in a vulnerable patient makes sepsis the overwhelmingly likely cause of shock.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with abdominal pain and vomiting.

demographics.text: 12-year-old male, previously healthy, losing weight recently.
25-year-old female with history of Type 1 DM, missed insulin.
40-year-old male, history of alcohol abuse.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Missing new-onset DKA in children (misdiagnosed as gastroenteritis); ignoring pancreatitis in alcoholics.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Weight loss in a child with GI symptoms is a massive red flag for new-onset Type 1 DM and DKA.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with sudden wheezing and hives.

demographics.text: 10-year-old boy with known peanut allergy, just ate a cookie.
30-year-old female with history of asthma, no known allergies.
50-year-old male, started new ACE inhibitor today.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Ignoring anaphylaxis in patients without a known allergy history; misdiagnosing ACE-inhibitor angioedema as simple asthma.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: A known trigger (peanuts) or a new medication (ACEi) dramatically shifts the probability toward anaphylaxis.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with dyspnea and leg swelling.

demographics.text: 75-year-old male with history of 3 prior MIs.
60-year-old female, 50-pack-year smoker, history of COPD.
40-year-old female, obese, recently started OCPs.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Missing HF in COPD patients (they often coexist); ignoring PE in young women with dyspnea and swelling.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Prior MI is the strongest predictor of HF probability, while smoking history favors COPD.

drillLibrary.scenario: A patient presents with dysuria and frequency.

demographics.text: 25-year-old female, sexually active, new partner.
80-year-old female with dementia and new confusion.
30-year-old male, no PMH.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Over-diagnosing UTI in young men (where STI is more likely); missing UTI as a cause of delirium in the elderly.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: UTI is rare in young men and should prompt a search for STIs, while in the elderly, it's the primary cause of acute confusion.

evaluationRubric.correct: Assigned probabilities are within 10% of the correct values for all 3 demographics.
evaluationRubric.partiallyCorrect: Assigned probabilities are within 20% of the correct values; or the 'most likely' diagnosis is correctly identified for all 3.
evaluationRubric.wrong: Assigned probabilities are off by > 20%; or the 'most likely' diagnosis is incorrect.

progressionStages.name: Guided Recognition
progressionStages.scaffolding: User is shown the 'Base Rate' for each diagnosis in the general population.

progressionStages.name: Unguided Application
progressionStages.scaffolding: No base rate hints; raw demographics only.

progressionStages.name: Pressured Performance
progressionStages.scaffolding: Raw demographics + 20-second time limit per case.

progressionStages.name: Expert Calibration
progressionStages.scaffolding: Includes 'Bayesian Update' clues (e.g., 'Test X is positive') that shift the probabilities.

adaptiveRules.prioritization: Prioritize cases where the user significantly over-estimated a rare condition.

masteryDefinition.speedBenchmark: Average < 10 seconds to assign probabilities for 3 demographics
masteryDefinition.consistency: Zero instances of 'Base Rate Neglect' in Stage 3
